summary: Gruber dismantles a TheNextWeb piece arguing Apple has reached 'peak Apple' and is headed for decline under Tim Cook. He systematically rebuts the claim that Apple's dependence on iPhone revenue signals trouble, showing that Apple's non-iPhone businesses alone rival the total revenue of Google or Microsoft. He challenges the narrative that iPad is 'immaterial' and Apple Watch is a 'flop' by comparing their revenues to entire competitor companies. The piece argues that Apple products succeed on merit, not cult-like devotion, and that critics apply a double standard where only iPhone-level success counts.
key_points: Apple's non-iPhone revenue ($19.3B/quarter) roughly equals all of Microsoft or Google, yet Apple is called dangerously dependent on one product while Google's 92% ad-revenue dependence goes unremarked
Arguing Apple is in trouble because iPhone is so dominant is like saying the '90s Bulls were in trouble because Jordan was too good — the success is real even if temporary
iPad generates more quarterly revenue than all of Google's non-advertising business does annually, yet it's dismissed as nearly immaterial
The 'cult of Apple' narrative is false — products like the G4 Cube and iPod Hi-Fi flopped, proving customers buy on merit, not blind loyalty

Critics demand Apple Watch compete with the ninth-generation iPhone as a first-gen product, and simultaneously argue Apple's car project faces insurmountable obstacles — the same arguments made about every industry Apple has entered
money_quotes: Arguing that Apple is in trouble because the iPhone is so popular is like arguing that the '90s-era Chicago Bulls were in trouble because Michael Jordan was so good.

Take away every single iPhone sold — all of them — and Apple's remaining business for the quarter was almost as big as Microsoft's, bigger than Google's, and more than four times the size of Facebook's.

92 percent of Google's revenue last year came from online advertising. And more importantly, I don't get why Apple's non-iPhone businesses are so quickly written off only because they're so much smaller than the iPhone.

Name a product introduced in the last five years that has been more successful than the iPad — either in terms of revenue and profit for its maker, or in terms of aggregate hours of daily use and customer satisfaction of its users.
Apple's business is making customers happy, and keeping them happy. They make products for discriminating people who have higher standards and less tolerance for design flaws or problems.
It's just as foolish to argue that Apple can't succeed — or that anything less than iPhone-sized success in a new endeavor is a failure.
If Apple's fall under Cook is much slower than its rise under Steve Jobs, it's going to take 20 or 30 years.
key_insight: The 'peak Apple' narrative applies a double standard where only iPhone-scale success counts, ignoring that Apple's non-iPhone businesses alone would rank among the world's largest tech companies.
